edges.description: Reduced HLCS function impairs biotinylation of carboxylase apoproteins.
Reduced biotinylation leaves multiple carboxylases in low-activity apo forms.
Defective biotin-dependent enzyme activation is associated with cutaneous and hair manifestations in HLCS deficiency.
PC dysfunction impairs pyruvate anaplerosis and glucose production.
intermediate_mechanisms: Impaired pyruvate carboxylase biotinylation and activity

edges.description: MCC dysfunction drives accumulation of leucine-pathway intermediates.
PCC dysfunction drives accumulation of propionyl-derived metabolites.
intermediate_mechanisms: Impaired propionyl-CoA carboxylase biotinylation and activity

edges.description: Impaired carbohydrate flux contributes to metabolic decompensation under catabolic stress.
intermediate_mechanisms: Impaired glucose production during catabolic stress
edges.target: Secondary organic acidemia and ketotic decompensation

edges.description: Pyruvate carboxylase-related impairment manifests clinically as lactic acidosis.
Reduced gluconeogenic capacity can present as hypoglycemia during illness or poor intake.
Lactate elevation is the biochemical counterpart of lactic acidosis.
Leucine-pathway metabolite accumulation contributes to acute metabolic crisis.
edges.target: Secondary organic acidemia and ketotic decompensation

edges.description: MCC pathway impairment increases the C5-OH acylcarnitine marker.
MCC pathway impairment increases urinary hydroxyisovalerate-class metabolites.
MCC pathway impairment increases urinary 3-methylcrotonylglycine.
Propionyl-derived metabolite accumulation contributes to decompensation.
edges.target: Secondary organic acidemia and ketotic decompensation

edges.description: PCC pathway impairment increases urinary 3-hydroxypropionate.
PCC pathway impairment increases urinary methylcitric acid.
Acute metabolic stress can secondarily impair nitrogen handling.
edges.source: Secondary organic acidemia and ketotic decompensation

edges.description: Stress-state management can unmask insulin secretion abnormalities.
intermediate_mechanisms: Secondary Krebs cycle disturbance in pancreatic beta cells
edges.source: Secondary organic acidemia and ketotic decompensation

edges.description: Acidotic decompensation, organic acid accumulation, hypoglycemia, and hyperammonemia can produce acute neurologic dysfunction.
edges.source: Secondary organic acidemia and ketotic decompensation

edges.description: Multisystem decompensation and chronic illness can include feeding difficulty and impaired growth.
edges.source: Secondary organic acidemia and ketotic decompensation

edges.description: Organic acid accumulation manifests clinically as metabolic acidosis.
edges.source: Secondary organic acidemia and ketotic decompensation

edges.description: Acute metabolic decompensation commonly presents with vomiting.
edges.source: Secondary organic acidemia and ketotic decompensation

edges.description: Severe acidosis can drive rapid breathing as respiratory compensation.
edges.source: Secondary organic acidemia and ketotic decompensation

edges.description: The secondary nitrogen-disposal disturbance manifests as elevated circulating ammonia.
Impaired glucose-stimulated insulin secretion can cause hyperglycemia during glucose infusion.
Insulinopenia during decompensation can produce ketoacidosis.
Severe metabolic decompensation can produce seizures.
Severe metabolic decompensation can reduce alertness.
Acute neurologic metabolic dysfunction can manifest as hypotonia.
Severe or delayed metabolic control can contribute to developmental delay.
The cutaneous involvement includes eczematous or erythematous rash.
The hair involvement includes alopecia.
Severe cutaneous involvement may include generalized ichthyosis.
Multisystem illness can present with feeding difficulties.
Chronic feeding and systemic involvement can manifest as growth retardation.
Increased C5-OH acylcarnitine reports impaired 3-methylcrotonyl-CoA carboxylase-dependent leucine catabolism.
Elevated lactate reports pyruvate carboxylase-related disruption of pyruvate handling, gluconeogenesis, and lactic acidosis.
Increased urinary 3-hydroxyisovaleric acid reports accumulation of leucine-pathway intermediates downstream of MCC dysfunction.
Increased urinary methylcitric acid reports propionyl-CoA carboxylase impairment and propionate-pathway metabolite accumulation.
Increased urinary 3-methylcrotonylglycine reports impaired 3-methylcrotonyl-CoA carboxylase-dependent leucine catabolism.
Increased urinary 3-hydroxypropionate reports impaired propionate catabolism downstream of propionyl-CoA carboxylase dysfunction.

evidence.reference_title: Case report: A case of holocarboxylase synthetase deficiency with respiratory tract as the initial symptom.
evidence.snippet: urine organic acid analysis revealed elevations in lactate, 3-hydroxybutyric acid, 3-hydroxyisovaleric acid, acetoacetic acid, 3-methylcrotonylglycine, and methylcitric acid.

readouts.interpretation: Increased urinary 3-hydroxyisovaleric acid reports accumulation of leucine-pathway intermediates downstream of MCC dysfunction.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical, biochemical, and genetic analysis of 28 Chinese patients with holocarboxylase synthetase deficiency.
evidence.snippet: The concentration of 3-hydroxyisovalerylcarnitine (C5-OH) in blood and pyruvate, 3-hydroxypropionate, methylcitric acid, 3-hydroxyvaleric acid, 3-methylcrotonylglycine in urine were increased greatly among affected individuals.

readouts.interpretation: Increased C5-OH acylcarnitine reports impaired 3-methylcrotonyl-CoA carboxylase-dependent leucine catabolism.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical, biochemical, and genetic analysis of 28 Chinese patients with holocarboxylase synthetase deficiency.
evidence.snippet: The concentration of 3-hydroxyisovalerylcarnitine (C5-OH) in blood and pyruvate, 3-hydroxypropionate, methylcitric acid, 3-hydroxyvaleric acid, 3-methylcrotonylglycine in urine were increased greatly among affected individuals.

readouts.interpretation: Increased urinary 3-hydroxypropionate reports impaired propionate catabolism downstream of propionyl-CoA carboxylase dysfunction.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical, biochemical, and genetic analysis of 28 Chinese patients with holocarboxylase synthetase deficiency.
evidence.snippet: The concentration of 3-hydroxyisovalerylcarnitine (C5-OH) in blood and pyruvate, 3-hydroxypropionate, methylcitric acid, 3-hydroxyvaleric acid, 3-methylcrotonylglycine in urine were increased greatly among affected individuals.

readouts.interpretation: Increased urinary 3-methylcrotonylglycine reports impaired 3-methylcrotonyl-CoA carboxylase-dependent leucine catabolism.

nodes.description: Hair loss is a reported clinical feature of HLCS deficiency, reflecting biotin deficiency at the level of hair follicle biology.

evidence.reference_title: Dramatic Clinical Improvement With Biotin Mega-Dose Therapy in a Neonate With Holocarboxylase Synthetase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Clinical manifestations include severe metabolic acidosis, hyperammonemia, tachypnea, skin rash, alopecia, feeding problems, hypotonia, developmental delay, seizures, and, in severe cases, death.

evidence.reference_title: Impaired glucose homeostasis and a novel HLCS pathogenic variant in holocarboxylase synthetase deficiency: a report of two cases and brief review.
evidence.snippet: Typical manifestations of HCSD include eczema, alopecia, lactic acidosis and hyperammonemia.

nodes.description: HLCS deficiency includes cutaneous and hair findings such as rash, alopecia, and sometimes generalized ichthyosis. The graph groups these findings as downstream consequences of impaired biotin-dependent enzyme activation while leaving the tissue-level intermediates unspecified.

evidence.reference_title: Dramatic Clinical Improvement With Biotin Mega-Dose Therapy in a Neonate With Holocarboxylase Synthetase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Clinical manifestations include severe metabolic acidosis, hyperammonemia, tachypnea, skin rash, alopecia, feeding problems, hypotonia, developmental delay, seizures, and, in severe cases, death.

evidence.reference_title: Dramatic Clinical Improvement With Biotin Mega-Dose Therapy in a Neonate With Holocarboxylase Synthetase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Despite receiving supportive care, no evident clinical improvement was observed, accompanied by the onset of generalized ichthyosis.

nodes.description: Pyruvate carboxylase (PC) deficiency due to impaired biotinylation reduces conversion of pyruvate to oxaloacetate, impairing gluconeogenesis and TCA cycle anaplerosis. This contributes to lactic acidosis and hypoglycemia observed in affected patients.

evidence.reference_title: Impaired glucose homeostasis and a novel HLCS pathogenic variant in holocarboxylase synthetase deficiency: a report of two cases and brief review.
evidence.snippet: Typical manifestations of HCSD include eczema, alopecia, lactic acidosis and hyperammonemia.

evidence.reference_title: Dramatic Clinical Improvement With Biotin Mega-Dose Therapy in a Neonate With Holocarboxylase Synthetase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: An 8-day-old female neonate presented with severe lactic acidosis, necessitating sedation and mechanical ventilation.

nodes.description: Poor feeding and feeding intolerance occur in neonatal and infantile presentations, contributing to failure to thrive.

evidence.reference_title: Dramatic Clinical Improvement With Biotin Mega-Dose Therapy in a Neonate With Holocarboxylase Synthetase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Clinical manifestations include severe metabolic acidosis, hyperammonemia, tachypnea, skin rash, alopecia, feeding problems, hypotonia, developmental delay, seizures

evidence.reference_title: Case report: A case of holocarboxylase synthetase deficiency with respiratory tract as the initial symptom.
evidence.snippet: Common clinical manifestations include metabolic acidosis, rash, feeding difficulties, and growth retardation

nodes.description: Developmental delay may occur, particularly in patients with delayed diagnosis or severe metabolic crises. Early biotin treatment is associated with normal developmental outcomes.

evidence.reference_title: Dramatic Clinical Improvement With Biotin Mega-Dose Therapy in a Neonate With Holocarboxylase Synthetase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Clinical manifestations include severe metabolic acidosis, hyperammonemia, tachypnea, skin rash, alopecia, feeding problems, hypotonia, developmental delay, seizures

evidence.reference_title: Clinical, biochemical, and genetic analysis of 28 Chinese patients with holocarboxylase synthetase deficiency.
evidence.snippet: After prompt supplement of biotin, both the clinical and biochemical symptoms were dramatically resolved and nearly all patients developed normal intelligence and physique on follow-up.

nodes.description: In some patients, secondary Krebs cycle disturbances may impair pancreatic beta-cell insulin secretion, leading to hyperglycemic ketoacidosis during glucose infusion therapy.

evidence.reference_title: Insulin therapy in acute decompensation of holocarboxylase synthetase deficiency with hyperglycemia and ketoacidosis.
evidence.snippet: Management with intravenous glucose aiming to stimulate anabolism led to hyperglycemic ketoacidosis. Insulin therapy rapidly corrected biochemical parameters, and clinical status improved.

evidence.reference_title: Insulin therapy in acute decompensation of holocarboxylase synthetase deficiency with hyperglycemia and ketoacidosis.
evidence.snippet: We propose that secondary Krebs cycle disturbances affecting pancreatic beta cells impaired glucose-stimulated insulin secretion, resulting in insulinopenia.

nodes.description: Growth retardation may occur in the setting of chronic metabolic disease and feeding difficulties.

evidence.reference_title: Case report: A case of holocarboxylase synthetase deficiency with respiratory tract as the initial symptom.
evidence.snippet: Common clinical manifestations include metabolic acidosis, rash, feeding difficulties, and growth retardation

nodes.description: The most common variant in a Chinese HLCS deficiency cohort, representing 41.1% of alleles. Located in the C-terminal biotin-binding domain of the HLCS protein.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical, biochemical, and genetic analysis of 28 Chinese patients with holocarboxylase synthetase deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Among them, the variant of c.1522C > T was the most common.

nodes.description: A pathogenic missense variant associated with early-onset severe neonatal presentation. Located in the N-terminal region of the HLCS protein.

evidence.reference_title: Dramatic Clinical Improvement With Biotin Mega-Dose Therapy in a Neonate With Holocarboxylase Synthetase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Genetic analysis of actionable metabolic disorders revealed compound heterozygous variants of HLCS (NM_000411.8), specifically c.[710T>C (p.Leu237Pro)]; [1544G>A (p.Ser515Asn)]

nodes.description: A novel pathogenic variant identified in homozygous state in siblings with very late onset HLCS deficiency, with symptomatic presentation at 11 years.

evidence.reference_title: Case report: Two siblings with very late onset of holocarboxylase synthase deficiency and a mini-review.
evidence.snippet: The younger sister presented at the age of 11 years and manifested as acute metabolic acidosis, which promptly resolved following rehydration and biotin administration.

evidence.reference_title: Case report: Two siblings with very late onset of holocarboxylase synthase deficiency and a mini-review.
evidence.snippet: genetic testing revealed a novel pathogenic variant in the HLCS gene (NM_000411.8) in the homozygous state: c.995A>G; p. (Gln332Arg).

nodes.description: Biallelic HLCS pathogenic variants reduce holocarboxylase synthetase catalytic activity.

evidence.snippet: Holocarboxylase synthetase deficiency (HLCS deficiency, OMIM #253270) is an exceedingly rare metabolic disorder resulting in multiple carboxylase deficiencies owing to impaired biotin cycle.

evidence.reference_title: Case report: Two siblings with very late onset of holocarboxylase synthase deficiency and a mini-review.
evidence.snippet: A mini-review of HCS deficiency with late onset (>1 year) or early onset (<1 month) revealed that splice variants are associated with late onset, while both variants p. (Leu216Arg) and p. (Leu237Pro) are associated with early onset.

nodes.description: Hyperammonemia occurs during metabolic decompensation, likely from catabolic stress and secondary urea cycle impairment.

nodes.description: HLCS covalently links biotin to apocarboxylases to generate active holocarboxylases. Deficiency impairs this biotinylation step, reducing activation of biotin-dependent carboxylases.
